inference_unit.problem: The paper addresses the algorithmic and combinatorial task of certifying and extracting sparsity-structure in finite graphs under the $(k,\ell)$ count condition: a graph is $(k,\ell)$-sparse if every nonempty vertex-induced subgraph with $n'$ vertices spans at most $kn'-\ell$ edges, and tight if it attains equality on the whole vertex set. This family of sparse graphs is central in combinatorial rigidity (e.g. Laman graphs for $(2,3)$-tightness) and in arboricity problems (Tutte–Nash–Williams). Prior combinatorial characterizations exist in restricted regimes: (i) the lower range $0\le\ell\le k$ is handled by matroid-union descriptions (maps-and-trees, augmenting-path algorithms) which yield both characterizations and implementable algorithms; (ii) the upper range $k\le\ell\le 2k-1$ admits combinatorial certificates (proper $\ell T k$) but the constructive, algorithmic extraction of such decompositions is less direct and matroid-augmenting-path techniques do not apply straightforwardly. Earlier pebble-game formulations produced recognition algorithms for sparsity but left open how to use pebble games to produce sparsity-certifying decompositions and to unify the lower and upper ranges algorithmically. Consequently, there was no single, simple, implementable combinatorial construction that (a) characterizes all $(k,\ell)$-sparse graphs across the matroidal range $0\le\ell\le 2k-1$, (b) yields explicit decompositions certifying sparsity (maps-and-trees or proper $\ell T k$), and (c) supports efficient (practically implementable) extraction algorithms with near-optimal time/space bounds. The absence of such a unifying, constructive method limited direct combinatorial proofs, practical implementations, and extension of lower-range augmenting-path speedups to the upper range.

conclusions.conclusion:
For integers $k\ge 0$ and $\ell$ with $0\le \ell\le 2k-1$, a finite (multi)graph $G$ is $(k,\ell)$-sparse (i.e. every non-empty vertex-induced subgraph on $n'$ vertices spans at most $kn'-\ell$ edges) if and only if $G$ is constructible by the $(k,\ell)$-pebble game with colors. Here the $(k,\ell)$-pebble game with colors is the constructive process that initializes $k$ labeled pebble colors $c_1,\dots,c_k$ on each vertex, and repeatedly applies two operations: (a) add-edge-with-colors: if the two endpoints together carry at least $\ell+1$ pebbles, remove one pebble from a chosen endpoint, insert the oriented edge $v\!w$ and place the removed pebble on that edge (the edge inherits the pebble's color); (b) pebble-slide: for a directed edge $v\!w$ where $w$ has a pebble, reverse the edge to $w\!v$, move the pebble formerly on $v\!w$ to $v$, and place the pebble from $w$ onto $w\!v$ (edge colors follow the pebbles). The underlying undirected graph produced by some finite sequence of these moves equals $G$ if and only if $G$ satisfies the $(k,\ell)$ sparsity counts.
Physical/scientific context: Researchers needed a constructive, combinatorial characterization of $(k,\ell)$-sparse graphs that is valid for the entire matroidal parameter range $0\le\ell\le 2k-1$, not only for special cases arising in rigidity or arboricity. Prior approaches and limitations: Existing combinatorial characterizations and algorithms were split: matroid-union and augmenting-path techniques provide both characterizations and efficient augmenting-path algorithms in the lower range $0\le\ell\le k$, while previous pebble-game formulations gave recognition algorithms but did not provide a pebble-game construction that is provably equivalent to the $(k,\ell)$ sparsity condition across the whole range. The upper range $k\le\ell\le 2k-1$ lacked a pebble-game-style constructive characterization, and matroid-augmenting-path methods do not extend naturally there. Consequences of the gap: Without a single constructive combinatorial model, there was no uniform way to (i) produce sparsity-certifying decompositions directly during construction, (ii) obtain unified combinatorial proofs linking the various decomposition certificates, and (iii) leverage the simplicity and implementability of pebble-game data-structures to produce efficient extraction algorithms across the entire matroidal range.

A graph $G$ is a pebble-game graph (i.e., constructible by the $(k,\ell)$-pebble game with colors) if and only if $G$ admits a partition of its edges into $k$ edge-disjoint subgraphs $H_1,\dots,H_k$ (the colors) such that: (i) each color subgraph $H_i$ is $(1,0)$-sparse (equivalently every nonempty vertex-induced subgraph on $n'$ vertices spans at most $1\cdot n' - 0 = n'$ edges, or equivalently $H_i$ admits an orientation with out-degree at most one at every vertex); and (ii) for every vertex-induced subgraph $G'=(V',E')$ of $G$, the collection of monochromatic tree-pieces of $G'$ (where a monochromatic tree-piece is a connected monochromatic component that is acyclic when restricted to $G'$) has cardinality at least $\ell$.
Physical/scientific context: For certification of $(k,\ell)$-sparsity one needs decompositions whose combinatorial properties exactly delineate the sparse family. Prior approaches and limitations: In the lower range, decompositions into map-graphs and trees (maps-and-trees) or matroid-union bases provided certificates; in the upper range, proper $\ell T k$ decompositions were known but their relation to constructive pebble-game processes and to decompositions built from simple monochromatic pieces was not formalized. The previous pebble-game formulations lacked an edge-coloring interpretation that yields a decomposition with uniform local properties per color. Consequences of the gap: Without such a decomposition-equivalence, one cannot read a sparsity certificate directly off a pebble-game construction in a uniform way across parameters; this obstructs both unified combinatorial understanding and direct algorithmic extraction of certificates during construction.

For integers with $0\le\ell\le k$, a graph $G$ on $n$ vertices is $(k,\ell)$-tight (i.e. $(k,\ell)$-sparse and $|E(G)|=kn-\ell$) if and only if $G$ admits a decomposition into $(k-\ell)$ edge-disjoint map-graphs (each map-graph is a graph admitting an orientation with out-degree exactly one everywhere) and $\ell$ edge-disjoint spanning trees (equivalently, $G$ is a $(k,\ell)$-maps-and-trees). Equivalently, tight pebble-game constructions produce exactly the maps-and-trees decompositions in the lower range.
Physical/scientific context: The lower-range $(k,\ell)$ tight graphs include classical arboricity cases and arise in rigidity contexts; practitioners need constructive decompositions into map-graphs and spanning trees that certify tightness. Prior approaches and limitations: Tutte and Nash–Williams provided existence characterizations of decompositions for certain parameters (e.g. spanning tree packings for $\ell=k$), and matroid-union methods and augmenting-path algorithms yield constructive extraction in the lower range, but a pebble-game-based constructive method producing maps-and-trees (and a direct combinatorial proof from pebble-game rules) was missing. Consequences of the gap: Without a pebble-game-based equivalence, there was no simple, unified pebble-game derivation of maps-and-trees decompositions nor a direct route to implementable pebble-game algorithms that output maps-and-trees; this separated the pebble-game recognition approach from certifying decompositions in practice.

conclusion.title: Upper-range equivalence: tight pebble-game graphs = proper ℓT k

conclusions.conclusion:
For integers with $k\le\ell\le 2k-1$, a graph $G$ on $n$ vertices is $(k,\ell)$-tight (i.e. $(k,\ell)$-sparse and $|E(G)|=kn-\ell$) if and only if $G$ admits a decomposition into $\ell$ edge-disjoint trees (not necessarily spanning) such that each vertex belongs to exactly $k$ of these $\ell$ trees and the decomposition is proper in the sense that for every vertex-induced subgraph $G'=(V',E')$ the set of tree-pieces induced in $G'$ has cardinality at least $\ell$. Equivalently, tight pebble-game constructions in the upper range yield exactly the proper $\ell T k$ decompositions.
Physical/scientific context: The upper-range $(k,\ell)$ tight graphs occur in generalizations of Laman-type rigidity theorems and in combinatorial decompositions studied by Crapo and Haas; practitioners require explicit decompositions certifying tightness that can be extracted algorithmically. Prior approaches and limitations: Combinatorial equivalences to proper $\ell T k$ were known (Crapo, Haas) but constructive extraction in a unified pebble-game framework and simple algorithmic procedures that produce such decompositions were lacking; matroid-union augmenting-path techniques do not generalize directly to this range. Consequences of the gap: Without a constructive pebble-game method that yields proper $\ell T k$ decompositions, there was a disconnect between existence proofs and implementable algorithms for certifying upper-range tightness, and no simple unified proof via pebble-game constructions.

conclusion.title: Existence of canonical pebble-game constructions (no unnecessary monochromatic cycles)

conclusions.conclusion:
For every graph $G$ that is constructible by the $(k,\ell)$-pebble game with colors, there exists a canonical pebble-game construction of $G$ that uses only (i) canonical add-edge moves (when adding an edge $vw$, color it by a color present on both $v$ and $w$ if possible, otherwise choose the highest-numbered color present) and (ii) canonical pebble-slide moves (pebble-slide moves are permitted only when they do not create a new monochromatic cycle). Such a canonical construction avoids creating unnecessary monochromatic cycles in the lowest $\min\{k,\ell\}$ colors and can be achieved by local "shortcut" transformations of arbitrary pebble-slide sequences.
Physical/scientific context: To translate pebble-game constructions into maps-and-trees or proper $\ell T k$ decompositions one needs constructions that minimize monochromatic cycles so that colors correspond to forests or to map-graphs as required. Prior approaches and limitations: Arbitrary pebble-game constructions can create monochromatic cycles in ways that obscure the correspondence to tree-based decompositions; there was no guarantee that from a given (possibly cyclic) pebble-game construction one could obtain one that yields the canonical decomposition-forms. Consequences of the gap: Without a canonical construction procedure, it was unclear how to systematically read maps-and-trees or proper $\ell T k$ decompositions off pebble-game outputs or to use pebble-game moves to simulate matroid-augmenting-path behavior in the upper range.

conclusion.title: O(n^2) algorithm to find canonical pebble-game constructions and decompositions

conclusions.conclusion:
There is an implementable algorithm (the canonical pebble game with colors together with a canonical path-finding subroutine) that, given an $n$-vertex input graph $G$, constructs a pebble-game graph $H$ that is a maximum-sized $(k,\ell)$-sparse subgraph of $G$ and outputs the pebble-induced $k$-color decomposition; when $G$ is tight the decomposition is either a $(k,\ell)$-maps-and-trees (lower range) or a proper $\ell T k$ (upper range) depending on parameters. The algorithm runs in time $O(n^2)$ and uses $O(n^2)$ space in the worst case: each accepted edge is processed in $O(n)$ time by the canonical path-finding routine (which converts an initial DFS path to a canonical sequence touching each edge at most a constant number of times), there are $O(n)$ accepted edges in a tight output, and rejected edges are processed in amortized $O(1)$ time using maintained component data-structures.
Physical/scientific context: For practical uses (e.g., rigidity testing, extracting spanning-tree certificates), an algorithm must produce sparsity-certifying decompositions efficiently and be implementable using simple data structures. Prior approaches and limitations: Matroid-union augmenting-path algorithms in the lower range achieve faster theoretical bounds ($O(n^{3/2})$) but require more complex data-structures and two subroutines (cyclic and batch scanning); naive canonical pebble-game implementations incur $O(n^3)$ time in the worst case because naive pebble-collection can perform $O(n)$ operations per pebble and there may be $O(n)$ pebbles to collect. Consequences of the gap: Without an $O(n^2)$ implementable algorithm, pebble-game characterizations could be of limited practical use for large graphs; practitioners lacked a simple, efficient method to extract decomposition certificates during a single constructive pass over edges.

conclusion.title: Unified combinatorial proofs of classical arboricity and tree-decomposition theorems

conclusions.conclusion:
The canonical pebble-game framework yields direct, unified combinatorial proofs of classical decomposition theorems: (i) for $0\le\ell\le k$, the Tutte–Nash–Williams arboricity theorem and related results (that tight graphs are exactly $(k,\ell)$-maps-and-trees) follow as corollaries of canonical pebble-game constructions; (ii) for $k\le\ell\le 2k-1$, the Crapo–Haas characterization (that tight graphs are exactly proper $\ell T k$ graphs) follows from canonical pebble-game constructions. These classical results are thus reproved in a single, constructive pebble-game framework.
Physical/scientific context: Classical theorems in graph decomposition (arboricity and tree-packing results) are fundamental but have disparate proofs and algorithmic interpretations. Prior approaches and limitations: Existing proofs used matroid theory, partition theorems, or case-by-case combinatorial arguments; no single constructive pebble-game-based proof produced both the decomposition and the constructive certificate uniformly across parameter ranges. Consequences of the gap: The absence of a unifying constructive proof limited transfer of pebble-game algorithmic simplicity and intuition to classical decomposition results and obstructed a compact constructive narrative linking arboricity, map-decompositions, and Laman-type tree-decompositions.

conclusion.title: Connection to slider-pinning: graded-tight graphs via pebble-to-loop replacement

conclusions.conclusion:
For the application-relevant case $(k,\ell)=(2,3)$, any graph produced by the $(2,3)$-pebble game with colors can be transformed into a graded-tight bar-slider combinatorial object by replacing pebbles by loops: specifically, replacing each pebble by a loop on its vertex (preserving colors) yields a graph that is (2,3)-sparse on loopless subgraphs and (2,0)-tight when loops are included; this property is summarized as the graph being $(2,0,3)$-graded-tight.
Physical/scientific context: Combinatorial models for bar-slider rigidity (frameworks with sliders modeled as loops) require a graded-sparsity notion that mixes constraints from plain edges and loops. Prior approaches and limitations: While graded-sparse matroid formulations existed, there was no simple constructive derivation linking pebble-game configurations to graded-tight combinatorial objects used in slider-pinning models; practitioners needed a straightforward mapping from constructive pebble outputs to graded-tight graphs. Consequences of the gap: Lacking such a mapping, it was harder to reuse pebble-game constructions to certify slider-pinning combinatorial rigidity or to extract loop placements from constructive procedures.

In the axis-parallel slider-pinning specialization (each slider is horizontal or vertical and is modeled as a pre-colored loop), any canonical $(2,3)$-pebble-game-with-colors tight graph, upon replacing pebbles by loops of the same color, yields the combinatorial graph of a minimally pinned axis-parallel bar-slider framework: equivalently, there exists a 2-coloring of edges so each color is a forest and each monochromatic tree in the decomposition spans exactly one loop of that color.
Physical/scientific context: For axis-parallel bar-slider rigidity, practitioners require a precise combinatorial certificate ensuring minimal pinning with colored sliders. Prior approaches and limitations: Existing graded-sparsity characterizations did not provide an immediately implementable way to obtain the required 2-color forests with loop-span properties from a simple constructive procedure. Consequences of the gap: Without a canonical constructive mapping, translating algorithmic sparse-graph extraction into certificate graphs for axis-parallel slider-pinning (useful in applications) remained ad hoc.

inference_unit.open_question: The authors identify two principal algorithmic open problems regarding improving pebble-game algorithms toward the best known theoretical bounds in the lower range and extending such improvements to the upper range: (1) develop a pebble-game algorithm that implements the batch-scanning capability used in Gabow and Westermann's $O(n^{3/2})$ matroid-union algorithm—i.e., a pebble-game analogue of batch scanning that finds many disjoint augmenting structures at once—yielding an implementable $O(n^{3/2})$ algorithm for the lower range $0\le\ell\le k$; and (2) extend batch scanning (or an analogous batched pebble-game mechanism) to the $\ell+1$ pebble condition used in the pebble game so as to derive an $O(n^{3/2})$ pebble-game algorithm for the upper range $k\le\ell\le 2k-1$. These open problems are explicitly stated as algorithmic directions: creating an implementable batch-scanning pebble-game subroutine and generalizing it to the $\ell+1$ pebble condition so that the faster $O(n^{3/2})$ time bound can be achieved for decomposition extraction in both ranges.
